Farn PolrrrcAr, PnACrrcns CoMMrssroN
-lll.l J Street . Suite 610 o Sacramento. CA 9581.1-l-129
(9I6t 3ll--5660 r Far (9l6) 321-0886
Scptember lO, ZOOT
Robert E. Murphy, City Attorney
City of West Sacramento
400 Capitol Mall, 27h Floor
Sacramento, CA 95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-07-147
Dear Mr. Murphy:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of West Sacramento
city councilmentber Mark Johannessen regarding the conflict-of-interest proririon, or
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' Nothing in-this letter should be construed to
evaluate any conduct that may have already tof", place, and any conclusions contained
in this letter apply only to prospective actions. tn addition, this letter is based on the facts
presented' The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission,,) does not act as a
finder of fact when it renders assistance. (rn re ogteibv (1975) l Fppc opr. ir.l
Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. we
therefore offer no opinion-on the application, if ory, oi other conflict-of-interest laws
such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.
QUESTTONS
l ' May councilmember Johannessen make. participate in making, or influence a
-qovernmental decision regarding a "package" of three proptsed developments despite his
property interest within 500 feer of two of ihe three propoied developmenrs? - -
' 'rhe Political Ret'orm Act is contained in Government Code Sections g1000 through 9l0l.l. All
stattlt()ryrc'f'erencesaretotheGovernmentCode.unlessotherwiseindicated. Theregulati.nsoftheFair
Political Practices Commission are eontainecl in See tions l8I l0 thnrugt tsqsz of ritle 2 of the Calitornia
code of Regulations' All regulakrry ref'erences are to Title 2. Divisioi 6 of the Calitirrnia code .f
Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.


File No. A-07-147
Page No. 2
2. May Councilmember Johannessen make, participate in making, or influence a
governmental decision to adopt a traffic model for two of the proposed developments
despite his property interest within 500 feet of one of the two developments?
3. May Councilmember Johannessen make, participate in making, or influence a
governmental decision regarding the City of West Sacramento's annexation policy and
sphere of influence study, inclusive of one of the proposed developments, deipite his
property interest within 500 feet of the development?
4. May Councilmember Johannessen make, participate in making, or influence a
governmental decision regarding a resolution requesting annexation, or development
entitlements, such as general plan amendments, specific plan amendments, zone changes,
and maps, for any of the three developments?
CONCLUSIONS
1-3. Councilmember Johannessen's economic interest in real property is directly
involved in the governmental decisions regarding the "package" of projeits, ihe traffic
model for the River Park and Yarborough developments, and the anneiation policy and
sphere of influence study. The financial effect of these decisions is presumed to b.
material. Accordingly, Councilmember Johannessen may not make, participate in
making, or influence these decisions unless he can prove that the prezumption of
materiality has been rebutted.
4. At this time, the governmental decisions regarding a resolution requesting
annexation or development entitlements are purely hypothetical. Without the factual
context of a specific decision, we are incapable of reaching any conclusions regarding
councilmember Johannessen's participation in these decisions.
FACTS
Mark Johannessen is a member of the West Sacramento City Council (the ..City
Council"). Councilmember Johannessen and his wife own an approximately four-acre
property within the City. This four-acre property is valued at more than $2,000.
In April 1996, the City of West Sacramento (the "City") adopted a master plan for
the southern portion of the City named the "southport Framework Pian (the "southport
Plan"). The essence of the Southport Plan was the development of five "villages" along a
new arterial loop road that circled most of the area. In recognition of the finite facilities
and desire to preserve low density in the area, the City Council adopted a resolution
requiring that staff formulate a review process for any additional development projects in
the Southport area.


File No. A-07-147
Page No. 3
The projected build out of the Southport Plan is 16,109 residential units. To date,
8,162 units have been built or approved. Three proposed developments known as
University ParWina Del Lago (2,2L3 units), Yarbrough (3,004 units), and River park
(2,788 units) are all located within the Southport area. If these three developments are
approved, there would be no additional units available for development under the
Southport Plan without an amendment to the plan. You anticipate that these three
developments will be presented to the City Council as a "package" of projects. Because
of limitations in traffic resources and other infrastructure, it appears unlikely that all three
developments will proceed simultaneously.
Councilmember Johannessen's four-acre property is within 500 feet of the
University Park/Vina Del Lago and Yarbrough developments. The property is also in the
vicinity, but beyond 500 feet from the River park development.
AI\ALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental deiision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's economic interesrs. (Section 87103; Regulation 1g700(a).) The
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, which
we apply to your question. (Regulation 18700(bXl)-(8).)
Step One: Is Councilmember Johannessen a,,public oflicial?,'
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(bXl).) A "public official" is "every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agenc y ...."
(Section 82048.) As a member of the City Council, Councilmember Johannessen is a
public official within the meaning of the Act.2
2 If a public official's office is listed in Section 87200 ("87200fi1ers" include members of a city
council) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting. then he or she
must: ( I ) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type oieconomic interest
involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in Regulation
18702.5(b)(lxB), on the record of the meeting: (2) recuse himself or herself: and (3) leave the room for rhe
duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars. absences and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in Regulation I g702.5,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)


File No. A-O7-147
Page No.4
Step Two: Is Councilmember Johannessen making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a govemmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant intervening substantive review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.3.) Councilmember Johannessen is making, participating in making,
or influencing a governmental decision when acting in his role as a City Council member
Step Three: What are Councilmember Johannessen's "economic interests?"
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
Section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
a
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which
he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703. 1(b));
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2):
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within l2 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c);
Regulation 18703.3);
a
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $390
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e); Regulation
18703.4);
An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule (Section 87103;
Regulation 18703.5).
o
a


File No. A-07-147
Page No. 5
From the facts submitted the only economic interest indicated is Councilmember
Johannessen's economic interest in real property. Because you have not provided any
facts indicating that any other economic interest may be affected, our analysis is limited
to councilmember Johannessen's economic interest in his real proper-ty.3
Step Four: Is Councilmember Johannessen,s economic interest directly or indirectly
involved in the decision?
Real Propertv:
Regulation 18704.2(a) states that real property in which a public official has an
economic interest is directlv involved in a governmental decision if any of the following
apply:
"(l) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any
part of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or
the proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the
governmental decision. . ..
"(Z)The govemmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or
exclusion from any city, county, district or other local governmental
subdivision, of the real property in which the official has an interest or a
similar decision affecting the real property ....
"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or
revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a
specific use or uses of the real property in which the official has an
interest.
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal, or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real property
in which the official has an interest.
"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to
select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area
committee, to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the redevelopment area, or to
rescind or amend any of the above decisions; and real property in which
I A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances. However, any
financial efTect a decision may have on Councilmember Johannessen's reol prop.rty is considered an effect
on his real property interests and would not be analyzed separately under thi "personal financial effects"
rules' (Reg. 18705.5(a).) Accordingly, the personal financial etfects rule does not appear to apply to
Councilmember Johannessen's circumstances and we will not discuss it further.


File No. A-07-147
Page No. 6
the official has an interest, or any part of it is located within the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the redevelopment area.
"(5) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to,
streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real
property in which the official has an interest will receive new or improved
services."
You have asked questions related to several governmental decisions that will be
faced by the city council. we discuss the govemmental decisions in turn.
1. The "package" ofprojects - From the facts provided, you expect the
University Park/Vina Del Lago, Yarbrough, and River Park develop-"ntr to come before
the City Council as a "package" of projects. Because Councilmemter Johannessen has a
property interest within 500 feet of the University Park/Vina Del Lago and yarbrough
developments, Councilmember Johannessen's economic interest in real property is
directly involved in the governmental decisions regarding the "package"^of projects.
2. The trffic model for the River Park and Yarborough developmenrs - From the
facts provided Councilmember Johannessen has a property interest witiin 500 feet of the
Yarbrough development. Thus, Councilmember Johannessen's economic interest in real
property is directly involved in the governmental decision regarding the "traffic model"
as the interest is within 500 feet of the property subject to the decision.
3. The City's annexation policy and sphere of influence studlt for lands south of
the City - From the fact's provided the City's annexation policy andsphere of influence
study will be inclusive of the University Park/Vina Del Lago development and
Councilmember Johannessen has a property interest within 500 feet of tn" development.
Accordingly, Councilmember Johannessen's economic interest in real property is directly
involved the governmental decisions regarding the City's annexation ptfi"y und th"
sphere of influence study.
4. Resolution requesting annexation, or development entitlements, such as
general plan amendments, specific plan amendments, zone changes, and maps_ At this
time' these decisions are purely hypothetical. Without the factual context of a specific
decision before the city council we are incapable of determining whether
Councilmember Johannessen's economic interest in real propert, is directly or indirectly
involved in the particular decision. Accordingly, *e prouide no further analysis of thesi
hypothetical decisions. Should Councilmember Johannessen need additionai assistance
regarding the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions as they relate to a specific decision
faced by the City Council, he should seek further advice providing alirelevant facts.


File No. A-07-147
Page No. 7
Materiality
Having identified the economic interest involved and determined that the interest
is directly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify the materiality
standard appropriate to Councilmember Johannessen's economic interest in real property.
The financial effect of a governmental decision on real property directly involved
in the govemmental decision is presumed to be marerial. lnegulation iaZOS.ZiaXl).)
This presumption may be rebutted only by proof that it is not ieasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have an1, financial effect on the property or, in other
words, not even a single penny's effect on the property.
Foreseeability
Once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is-,,reasonably
foreseeable" that the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeatle," but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner (1975) I Fppc ops. 19g.)
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, sttpra,
at 198.) Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providin g advice (In
re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), the foreseeability of a particulai financial effect is a
determination that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public
official.
ln terms of the govemmental decisions regarding the "package" of projects, the
traffic model for the River Park and Yarborough developmenti, and the Ciiy's annexation
policy and sphere of influence study for lands south of the City, you have indicated a
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Councilmember Johannessen's economic
interest in real property. While ultimately the determination of whether the financial
effect is material must be left up to Councilmember Johannessen's informed judgment,
the financial effect on Councilmember Johannessen's economic interest in his real
property is presumed to be material because the property is within 500 feet of the
property subject to these govemmental decisions. Accordingly, Councilmember
Johannessen may not make, participate in making, or influence governmental decisions
regarding the "package" of projects unless he can rebut the presumption of materiality.


File No. A-07-147
Page No. 8
Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?
Segmentation
Your letter presents two issues regarding the potential segmentation of clecisions.
The "Package" of Projects:
From the facts you have provided, it appears that a governmental decision
regarding any one of the three developments will impact the governmental decisions
relating to the other two developments because all three developments are limited by
traffic resources and infrastructure. Accordingly, it appears that a decision relating io uny
one of the three developments submitted as part of the "package" of projects will act to
determine, affirm, nullify, or alter any decision related to the other two developments.
Therefore, the "package" of projects decision is "inextricably interrelated" and cannot be
segmented. (Regulation 18709(b).)
Future Decisions:
Note that it may be possible to segment future decisions, so long as the decisions
are not "inextricably interrelated." The Commission has advised that "[u]nder certain
circumstances, a public official disqualified from one decision may participate in other
related decisions provided that the official's participation does not affect the decision in
which he or she has a conflict of interest." (1n re owen (1976) 2 Fppc ops. 77.)
Regulation 18709 provides the mles for "segmentation" of a governmental decision:
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official
has a financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all
of the following conditions apply:
(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be
broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated
to the decision in which the official has a disqualifying financial inrerest;
(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is
segmented from the other decisions;
(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interesr is
considered first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the
disqualified official's parriciparion in any way; and


File No. A-07-147
Page No. 9
(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial inrerest
has been made, the disqualified public official's participation does not
result in a reopening of, or otherwise financially affect, the decision from
which the official was disqualified.
(b) For pLlrposes of this regulation, decisions are "inextricably
interrelated" when the result of one decision will effectively determine.
aftirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision."a
Public Generally & Legally Required participation
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87 103 ; Regulation I 8707(a).)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to
take part in a decision despite the fact that the officiai may have a disqualifying conflict of
interest under the "legally required participation" excepti;n. This "*ciption afiplies only in
certain very specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed fiom
acting. (Section 87101; Regulation 18708.)
You have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally" or the
"legally required participation" exceptions are applicable to Councilmembei
Johannessen's circumstances, so we will not address them further.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (gL6) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division
BGL:jgl
r You have provided no indications that the governmental decisions regarding the tratfic model
for the River Park and Yarborough developments. or the City's annexation polii and iphere of influence
study ftlr lands south of the City. can be segmented. Although. we note thai it may be possible to segmenr
future decisions as discussed. If circumstances indicate a po.ssibility of segmenting decisions,
Councilmember Johannessen should seek turther advice regarding ihe application-of ,.g..rtation to the
particular circumstances providing all relevant facts.

governmental decision to adopt a traffic model for two of the proposed developments
despite his property interest within 500 feet of one of the two developments?
3. May Councilmember Johannessen make, participate in making, or influence a
governmental decision regarding the City of West Sacramento's annexation policy and
sphere of influence study, inclusive of one of the proposed developments, deipite his
property interest within 500 feet of the development?
4. May Councilmember Johannessen make, participate in making, or influence a
governmental decision regarding a resolution requesting annexation, or development
entitlements, such as general plan amendments, specific plan amendments, zone changes,
and maps, for any of the three developments?
sections.conclusion:
1-3. Councilmember Johannessen's economic interest in real property is directly
involved in the governmental decisions regarding the "package" of projeits, ihe traffic
model for the River Park and Yarborough developments, and the anneiation policy and
sphere of influence study. The financial effect of these decisions is presumed to b.
material. Accordingly, Councilmember Johannessen may not make, participate in
making, or influence these decisions unless he can prove that the prezumption of
materiality has been rebutted.
4. At this time, the governmental decisions regarding a resolution requesting
annexation or development entitlements are purely hypothetical. Without the factual
context of a specific decision, we are incapable of reaching any conclusions regarding
councilmember Johannessen's participation in these decisions.

Mark Johannessen is a member of the West Sacramento City Council (the ..City
Council"). Councilmember Johannessen and his wife own an approximately four-acre

and desire to preserve low density in the area, the City Council adopted a resolution

The projected build out of the Southport Plan is 16,109 residential units. To date,
8,162 units have been built or approved. Three proposed developments known as
University ParWina Del Lago (2,2L3 units), Yarbrough (3,004 units), and River park

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental deiision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's economic interesrs. (Section 87103; Regulation 1g700(a).) The
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, which
we apply to your question. (Regulation 18700(bXl)-(8).)
Step One: Is Councilmember Johannessen a,,public oflicial?,'
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(bXl).) A "public official" is "every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agenc y ...."
(Section 82048.) As a member of the City Council, Councilmember Johannessen is a
public official within the meaning of the Act.2
2 If a public official's office is listed in Section 87200 ("87200fi1ers" include members of a city
council) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting. then he or she
must: ( I ) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type oieconomic interest
involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in Regulation
18702.5(b)(lxB), on the record of the meeting: (2) recuse himself or herself: and (3) leave the room for rhe
duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars. absences and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in Regulation I g702.5,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)


File No. A-O7-147
Step Two: Is Councilmember Johannessen making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a govemmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant intervening substantive review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.3.) Councilmember Johannessen is making, participating in making,
or influencing a governmental decision when acting in his role as a City Council member
Step Three: What are Councilmember Johannessen's "economic interests?"
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
Section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
a
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which
he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703. 1(b));
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2):
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within l2 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c);
Regulation 18703.3);
a
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $390
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e); Regulation
18703.4);
An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule (Section 87103;
Regulation 18703.5).
o
a
From the facts submitted the only economic interest indicated is Councilmember
Johannessen's economic interest in real property. Because you have not provided any
facts indicating that any other economic interest may be affected, our analysis is limited
to councilmember Johannessen's economic interest in his real proper-ty.3
Step Four: Is Councilmember Johannessen,s economic interest directly or indirectly
involved in the decision?
Real Propertv:
Regulation 18704.2(a) states that real property in which a public official has an
economic interest is directlv involved in a governmental decision if any of the following
apply:
"(l) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any
part of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or
the proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the
governmental decision. . ..

annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or
exclusion from any city, county, district or other local governmental
subdivision, of the real property in which the official has an interest or a
similar decision affecting the real property ....
"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or
revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a
specific use or uses of the real property in which the official has an
interest.
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal, or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real property
in which the official has an interest.
"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to
select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area

property in which the official has an interest will receive new or improved
services."
You have asked questions related to several governmental decisions that will be
faced by the city council. we discuss the govemmental decisions in turn.
1. The "package" ofprojects - From the facts provided, you expect the
University Park/Vina Del Lago, Yarbrough, and River Park develop-"ntr to come before
the City Council as a "package" of projects. Because Councilmemter Johannessen has a
property interest within 500 feet of the University Park/Vina Del Lago and yarbrough
developments, Councilmember Johannessen's economic interest in real property is
directly involved in the governmental decisions regarding the "package"^of projects.
2. The trffic model for the River Park and Yarborough developmenrs - From the
facts provided Councilmember Johannessen has a property interest witiin 500 feet of the
Yarbrough development. Thus, Councilmember Johannessen's economic interest in real
property is directly involved in the governmental decision regarding the "traffic model"
as the interest is within 500 feet of the property subject to the decision.
3. The City's annexation policy and sphere of influence studlt for lands south of
the City - From the fact's provided the City's annexation policy andsphere of influence
study will be inclusive of the University Park/Vina Del Lago development and
Councilmember Johannessen has a property interest within 500 feet of tn" development.
Accordingly, Councilmember Johannessen's economic interest in real property is directly
involved the governmental decisions regarding the City's annexation ptfi"y und th"
sphere of influence study.
4. Resolution requesting annexation, or development entitlements, such as
general plan amendments, specific plan amendments, zone changes, and maps_ At this
time' these decisions are purely hypothetical. Without the factual context of a specific
decision before the city council we are incapable of determining whether
Councilmember Johannessen's economic interest in real propert, is directly or indirectly
involved in the particular decision. Accordingly, *e prouide no further analysis of thesi
hypothetical decisions. Should Councilmember Johannessen need additionai assistance
regarding the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions as they relate to a specific decision
faced by the City Council, he should seek further advice providing alirelevant facts.
Materiality
Having identified the economic interest involved and determined that the interest
is directly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify the materiality
standard appropriate to Councilmember Johannessen's economic interest in real property.
The financial effect of a governmental decision on real property directly involved
in the govemmental decision is presumed to be marerial. lnegulation iaZOS.ZiaXl).)
This presumption may be rebutted only by proof that it is not ieasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have an1, financial effect on the property or, in other
words, not even a single penny's effect on the property.
Foreseeability
Once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is-,,reasonably
foreseeable" that the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeatle," but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner (1975) I Fppc ops. 19g.)
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, sttpra,
at 198.) Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providin g advice (In
re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), the foreseeability of a particulai financial effect is a
determination that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public
official.
ln terms of the govemmental decisions regarding the "package" of projects, the
traffic model for the River Park and Yarborough developmenti, and the Ciiy's annexation
policy and sphere of influence study for lands south of the City, you have indicated a
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Councilmember Johannessen's economic
interest in real property. While ultimately the determination of whether the financial
effect is material must be left up to Councilmember Johannessen's informed judgment,
the financial effect on Councilmember Johannessen's economic interest in his real
property is presumed to be material because the property is within 500 feet of the
property subject to these govemmental decisions. Accordingly, Councilmember
Johannessen may not make, participate in making, or influence governmental decisions
regarding the "package" of projects unless he can rebut the presumption of materiality.
Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?
Segmentation
Your letter presents two issues regarding the potential segmentation of clecisions.
The "Package" of Projects:
From the facts you have provided, it appears that a governmental decision
regarding any one of the three developments will impact the governmental decisions
relating to the other two developments because all three developments are limited by
traffic resources and infrastructure. Accordingly, it appears that a decision relating io uny
one of the three developments submitted as part of the "package" of projects will act to
determine, affirm, nullify, or alter any decision related to the other two developments.
Therefore, the "package" of projects decision is "inextricably interrelated" and cannot be
segmented. (Regulation 18709(b).)
Future Decisions:
Note that it may be possible to segment future decisions, so long as the decisions
are not "inextricably interrelated." The Commission has advised that "[u]nder certain
circumstances, a public official disqualified from one decision may participate in other
related decisions provided that the official's participation does not affect the decision in
which he or she has a conflict of interest." (1n re owen (1976) 2 Fppc ops. 77.)
Regulation 18709 provides the mles for "segmentation" of a governmental decision:
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official
has a financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all
of the following conditions apply:
(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be
broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated
to the decision in which the official has a disqualifying financial inrerest;
(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is
segmented from the other decisions;
(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interesr is
considered first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the
disqualified official's parriciparion in any way; and
(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial inrerest
has been made, the disqualified public official's participation does not
result in a reopening of, or otherwise financially affect, the decision from
which the official was disqualified.
(b) For pLlrposes of this regulation, decisions are "inextricably
interrelated" when the result of one decision will effectively determine.
aftirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision."a
Public Generally & Legally Required participation
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87 103 ; Regulation I 8707(a).)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to
take part in a decision despite the fact that the officiai may have a disqualifying conflict of
interest under the "legally required participation" excepti;n. This "*ciption afiplies only in
certain very specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed fiom
acting. (Section 87101; Regulation 18708.)
You have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally" or the
"legally required participation" exceptions are applicable to Councilmembei
Johannessen's circumstances, so we will not address them further.
